text: The surgeon performs a robotic cholecystectomy on a larger patient, adjusting the position to reverse Trendelenburg for better visualization. The surgical ports are placed above the umbilicus, and the surgeon carefully dissects inflammatory adhesions from the bottom of the gallbladder amidst visceral fat.

After achieving better retraction and visualization, the surgeon begins dissection at the neck of the gallbladder, which appears edematous and thickened. The tissue is visibly vascular, suggesting possible occlusion of the cystic duct.

The surgeon continues dissection higher on the gallbladder, encountering difficulty with the left robotic arm due to its placement near the right rib cage. Despite this, the dissection progresses, exposing the cystic artery.

The surgeon attempts to visualize the cystic duct and the bottom of the gallbladder, using ICG for anatomical clarity. A venous branch or vessel is inadvertently injured on the back of the cystic duct, resulting in bleeding within the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully creates space on the cystic duct to place clips, aiming to stop bleeding from vessels on the duct. Multiple instruments are used, including a suction device, due to unexpected bleeding. The gallbladder is being detached from the liver, with the surgeon separating a posterior branch of the cystic artery from the gallbladder.

The surgeon uses a top-down approach to reach the remaining attachments of the gallbladder. The final view shows the cystic artery and duct, with the common bile duct visible posteriorly. The surgeon checks for any bleeding from previously dissected areas.
